evidence_text: The study analyzes 567 pull requests generated using Claude Code across 157 open-source projects.
citation: Watanabe et al. 2025, Abstract and Section 3
source_excerpt: The abstract states, “we empirically study 567 GitHub pull requests (PRs) generated using Claude Code ... across 157 diverse open-source projects.” Section 3 says, “In the end, both datasets have 567 PRs across 157 repositories.”
note:
The paper frames these PRs as explicitly Claude Code-generated artifacts rather than a general sample of all AI-assisted pull requests.
```
caveat:
The paper frames these PRs as explicitly Claude Code-generated artifacts rather than a general sample of all AI-assisted pull requests.
```

evidence_text: Agentic PRs were identified by searching GitHub PR descriptions for the string “Generated with Claude Code” and restricting the search to repositories with at least 10 stars.
source_excerpt: Section 3 says, “To identify these Agentic-PRs, we searched for those containing the comment string ‘Generated with Claude Code’ in their descriptions” and “we limited this search to repositories with at least 10 GitHub stars.”
note:
The identification method depends on explicit provenance text in PR descriptions.
```
caveat:
The identification method depends on explicit provenance text in PR descriptions.
```

evidence_text: The search window for Agentic-PRs covered PRs submitted from February 24, 2025 to April 30, 2025.
source_excerpt: Section 3 says, “We conducted this search using the GitHub GraphQL API, focusing on PRs submitted between February 24, 2025 (the release date of Claude Code) and April 30, 2025 (the date our study began).”
note:
The sampled period is short and begins at Claude Code’s release.
```
caveat:
The sampled period is short and begins at Claude Code’s release.
```

evidence_text: 83.77425% of Agentic-PRs were accepted and merged, corresponding to 475 merged PRs out of 567.
citation: Watanabe et al. 2025, Abstract and Table 2
source_excerpt: The abstract says, “83.77425% of these agent-assisted PRs are eventually accepted and merged by project maintainers.” Table 2 reports “APRs 475 accepted ... 567 total ... 83.77425% acceptance.”
note:
The paper defines accepted PRs as merged PRs and rejected PRs as closed-but-unmerged PRs.
```
caveat:
The paper defines accepted PRs as merged PRs and rejected PRs as closed-but-unmerged PRs.
```

evidence_text: Agentic-PRs had a lower acceptance rate than Human-PRs: 83.77425% versus 91.00529%.
citation: Watanabe et al. 2025, RQ2 Results and Table 2
source_excerpt: RQ2 reports that “83.77425% of Agentic-PRs are accepted, though this rate is lower than Human-PRs (91.00529%).” Table 2 lists 475 of 567 APRs merged and 516 of 567 HPRs merged.
note:
The paper states that a chi-squared test found the higher Human-PR acceptance rate statistically significant.
```
caveat:
The paper states that a chi-squared test found the higher Human-PR acceptance rate statistically significant.
```

evidence_text: Median time to merge was 1.23 hours for Agentic-PRs and 1.04 hours for Human-PRs, with no significant difference in merge times.
citation: Watanabe et al. 2025, RQ2 Results and Table 2
source_excerpt: RQ2 states, “a log-rank test shows no significant difference in merge times between Agentic-PRs and Human-PRs.” Table 2 reports median time to merge of “1.23 hours” for APRs and “1.04 hours” for HPRs.
note:
The finding is about review-time distributions, not code quality directly.
```
caveat:
The finding is about review-time distributions, not code quality directly.
```

evidence_text: 54.94737% of merged Agentic-PRs were accepted without revision, corresponding to 261 PRs merged as a single-commit submission.
citation: Watanabe et al. 2025, Abstract and RQ3 Results
source_excerpt: The abstract says, “54.94737% of the merged PRs are integrated without further modification.” RQ3 states, “54.94737% of APRs (261 PRs) are merged as-is with a single commit.”
note:
The paper operationalizes “without revision” as merged with only the initial submission commit.
```
caveat:
The paper operationalizes “without revision” as merged with only the initial submission commit.
```

evidence_text: The share of merged PRs accepted without revision was similar for Agentic-PRs and Human-PRs: 54.94737% versus 58.52713%, with no statistically significant difference.
source_excerpt: RQ3 states, “54.94737% of APRs (261 PRs) are merged as-is ... as compared to 58.52713% (302 PRs) of HPRs. In addition, the chi-squared test revealed no statistically significant difference between the two groups.”
note:
This evidence indicates similarity on this metric despite the lower overall acceptance rate for Agentic-PRs.
```
caveat:
This evidence indicates similarity on this metric despite the lower overall acceptance rate for Agentic-PRs.
```

evidence_text: When revisions were required, the study found no statistically significant difference between Agentic-PRs and Human-PRs in revision frequency or measured revision cost.
source_excerpt: RQ3 says, “The Mann-Whitney U tests show no statistically significant differences between Agentic-PRs and Human-PRs in terms of revision frequency,” and that differences in “the number of files, lines added, and lines deleted” were also not statistically significant.
note:
The reported revision-cost metrics were commits, files changed, lines added/deleted, and percentage of code modification relative to the initial submission.
```
caveat:
The reported revision-cost metrics were commits, files changed, lines added/deleted, and percentage of code modification relative to the initial submission.
```

evidence_text: Refactoring appeared more often in Agentic-PRs than in Human-PRs: 24.9% versus 14.9%.
citation: Watanabe et al. 2025, Table 1 and RQ1 Results
source_excerpt: Table 1 lists “refactor” at “24.9% APRs” and “14.9% HPRs.” RQ1 states, “Code refactoring is more frequent in Agentic-PRs than Human-PRs.”
note:
The paper interprets these PRs as restructuring code to improve maintainability without changing behavior.
```
caveat:
The paper interprets these PRs as restructuring code to improve maintainability without changing behavior.
```

evidence_text: Documentation-related PRs were more common in Agentic-PRs than Human-PRs: 22.1% versus 14.0%.
citation: Watanabe et al. 2025, Table 1 and RQ1 Results
source_excerpt: Table 1 lists “docs” at “22.1% APRs” and “14.0% HPRs.” RQ1 states, “Documentation updates are more frequent in Agentic-PRs (22.1%) than in Human-PRs (14.0%).”
note:
The source says these documentation changes include user-facing documentation, code comments, and onboarding materials.
```
caveat:
The source says these documentation changes include user-facing documentation, code comments, and onboarding materials.
```

evidence_text: Test-related PRs were more common in Agentic-PRs than Human-PRs: 18.8% versus 4.5%.
citation: Watanabe et al. 2025, Table 1 and RQ1 Results
source_excerpt: Table 1 lists “test” at “18.8% APRs” and “4.5% HPRs.” RQ1 says, “Test-related improvements are substantially more common in Agentic-PRs than in Human-PRs (18.8% versus 4.5%).”
note:
The source describes these as additions or modifications to test files and test cases.
```
caveat:
The source describes these as additions or modifications to test files and test cases.
```

evidence_text: Multi-purpose PRs were more common in Agentic-PRs than Human-PRs: 40.0% versus 12.2%.
source_excerpt: RQ1 states, “Among 213 Agentic-PRs, 85 Agentic-PRs (40.0%) have multiple objectives, compared to just 27 multi-purpose Human-PRs (12.2%).”
note:
The source says common label pairings included feature plus test, refactor plus test, and bug fix plus test.
```
caveat:
The source says common label pairings included feature plus test, refactor plus test, and bug fix plus test.
```

evidence_text: Initial Agentic-PR submissions added more lines and had longer descriptions than Human-PRs, with medians of 48 versus 24 added lines and 355 versus 56 words in PR descriptions.
source_excerpt: RQ1 states, “both Agentic-PRs and Human-PRs modify a median of 2 files. APRs add more lines (48 vs 24) while maintaining similar deletion counts (7 vs 8). Moreover, APRs contain significantly longer PR descriptions with a median of 355 words, compared to 56 words in HPRs.”
note:
The paper says Mann-Whitney U tests confirmed the description-length difference was statistically significant.
```
caveat:
The paper says Mann-Whitney U tests confirmed the description-length difference was statistically significant.
```

evidence_text: Among rejected Agentic-PRs, the most frequently identified explicit rejection categories were alternative solutions by others or the team (12.1%), submission for verification only (5.5%), oversized PRs (3.3%), and obsolete PRs (3.3%).
citation: Watanabe et al. 2025, Table 3 and RQ2 Results
source_excerpt: RQ2 says, “The most common reasons for Agentic-PR rejections stem from project evolution and PR complexity,” and Table 3 lists “Are implementated by other PRs/developers 12.1%,” “Submission for verification 5.5%,” “Are too large 3.3%,” and “Are obsolete 3.3%.”
note:
This evidence is bounded because the rejection taxonomy depends on manual classification of rejection cases and many rejected PRs lacked explanatory feedback.
```
caveat:
This evidence is bounded because the rejection taxonomy depends on manual classification of rejection cases and many rejected PRs lacked explanatory feedback.
```

evidence_text: 63.7% of rejected Agentic-PRs were closed without explanatory comments or discussion.
citation: Watanabe et al. 2025, Table 3 and RQ2 Results
source_excerpt: Table 3 lists “Unknown (No feedback provided) 63.7%.” RQ2 states, “The majority (63.7%) of rejected Agentic-PRs were closed without explanatory comments or discussion,” and “58 out of 91 rejected APRs fell into this category.”
note:
This creates a visible limitation on interpreting rejection causes, because most rejected cases lacked explicit rationale in the captured review record.
```
caveat:
This creates a visible limitation on interpreting rejection causes, because most rejected cases lacked explicit rationale in the captured review record.
```

evidence_text: Among revised merged Agentic-PRs, the most common revision categories were bug fixes (45.1%), documentation updates (27.4%), refactoring (25.7%), and style changes (22.1%).
citation: Watanabe et al. 2025, Table 4 and RQ4 Results
source_excerpt: RQ4 states, “The majority (45.1%) of revisions to Agentic-PRs target bug fixes.” Table 4 lists “fix 45.1%,” “docs 27.4%,” “refactor 25.7%,” and “style 22.1%.”
note:
This evidence is bounded because the reported categories describe what revisions were applied, not a complete causal account of why each initial submission required those changes.
```
caveat:
This evidence is bounded because the reported categories describe what revisions were applied, not a complete causal account of why each initial submission required those changes.
```

evidence_text: 41.1% of revised Agentic-PRs were co-authored with Claude during revision, but the paper also states that the sample is limited to 567 explicitly labeled Claude Code PRs and may not generalize to other agentic coding tools.
citation: Watanabe et al. 2025, RQ4 Results and Threats to External Validity
source_excerpt: RQ4 states, “41.1% (88 out of 214) of the revised Agentic-PRs were co-authored with Claude.” Threats to external validity state, “This study examines only 567 PRs that explicitly used Claude Code” and “our findings may not generalize to other agentic coding tools.”
note: This evidence joins a measured revision-pattern result with the paper’s stated scope limitation, which constrains how broadly the observed pattern can be applied downstream.
caveat: This evidence joins a measured revision-pattern result with the paper’s stated scope limitation, which constrains how broadly the observed pattern can be applied downstream.
